lineage: Life and Works of Rufinus with Jerome's Apology Against Rufinus.
Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
I had praised Eusebius as well as Origen only as writers; and was forced to condemn them as heretics. Why should this be taken amiss?

title: I had praised Eusebius as well as Origen only as writers; and was forced to condemn them as heretics. Why should this be taken amiss?

source.title: NPNF2-03. Theodoret, Jerome, Gennadius, & Rufinus: Historical Writings

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Eusebius the Bishop of Cæsarea, of whom I have made mention above, in the sixth book of his Apology for Origen makes the same complaint against Methodius the bishop and martyr, which you make against me in your praises of me.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: He says: How could Methodius dare to write now against Origen, after having said this thing and that of his doctrines?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: This is not the place in which to speak of the martyr; one cannot discuss every thing in all places alike.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Let it suffice for the present to mention that one who was an Arian complains of the same things in a most eminent and eloquent man, and a martyr, which you first make a subject of praise as a friend and afterwards, when offended turn into an accusation.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I have given you an opportunity of constructing a calumny against me if you choose, in the present passage.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: “How is it,” you may ask, “that I now depreciate Eusebius, after having in other places praised him?”

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: The name Eusebius indeed is different from Origen; but the ground of complaint is in both cases identical.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I praised Eusebius for his Ecclesiastical History, for his Chronicle, for his description of the holy land; and these works of his I gave to the men of the same language as myself by translating them into Latin.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Am I to be called an Arian because Eusebius, the author of those books, is an Arian?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: If you should dare to call me a heretic, call to mind your Preface to the Περὶ ᾽Αρχῶν, in which you bear me witness that I am of the same faith with yourself: and I at the same time entreat you to hear patiently the expostulation of one who was formerly your friend.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: You enter into a warm dispute with others, and bandy mutual reproaches with men of your own order; whether you are right or wrong in this is for you to say.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But as against a brother even a true accusation is repugnant to me.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I do not say this to blame others; I only say that I would not myself do it.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: We are separated from one another by a vast interval of space.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: What sin had I committed against you?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: What is my offence?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Is it that I answered that I was not an Origenist?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Are you to be held to be accused because I defend myself?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: If you say you are not an Origenist and have never been one, I believe your solemn affirmation of this: if you once were one, I accept your repentance.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Why do you complain if I am what you say that you are?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Or is my offence this that I dared to translate the Περὶ ᾽Αρχῶν after you had done it, and that my translation is supposed to detract from your work?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But what was I to do?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Your laudation of me, or accusation against me, was sent to me.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Your ‘praise’ was so strong and so long that, if I had acquiesced in it, every one would have thought me a heretic.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Look at what is said in the end of the letter which I received from Rome: “Clear yourself from the suspicions which men have imbibed against you, and convict your accuser of speaking falsely; for if you leave him unnoticed, you will be held to assent to his charges.”

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: When I was pressed by such conditions, I determined to translate these books, and I ask your attention to the answer which I made.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: It was this: “This is the position which my friends have made for me, (observe that I did not say ‘my friend,’ for fear of seeming to aim at you); if I keep silence I am to be accounted guilty: if I answer, I am accounted an enemy.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Both these conditions are hard; but of the two I will choose the easier: for a quarrel can be healed, but blasphemy admits of no forgiveness.”

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: You observe that I felt this as a burden laid upon me; that I was unwilling and recalcitrating; that I could only quiet my presentiment of the quarrel which would ensue from this undertaking by the plea of necessity.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: If you had translated the books Περὶ ᾽Αρχῶν without alluding to me, you would have a right to complain that I had afterwards translated them to your prejudice.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But now you have no right to complain, since my work was only an answer to the attack you had made on me under the guise of praise; for what you call praise all understand as accusation.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Let it be understood between us that you accused me, and then you will not be indignant at my having replied.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But now suppose that you wrote with a good intention, that you were not merely innocent but a most faithful friend, out of whose mouth no untruth ever proceeded, and that it was quite unconsciously that you wounded me.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: What is that to me who felt the wound?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Am I not to take remedies for my wound because you inflicted it without evil intention?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I am stricken down and stricken through, with a wound in the breast which will not be appeased; my limbs which were white before are stained with gore; and you say to me: “Pray leave your wound untouched, for fear that I may be thought to have wounded you.”

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: And yet the translation in question is a reproof to Origen rather than to you.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: You altered for the better the passages which you considered to have been put in by the heretics.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I brought to light what the whole Greek world with one voice attributes to him.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Which of our two views is the truer it is not for me nor for you to judge; let each of them be touched by the censor’s rod of the reader.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: The whole of that letter in which I make answer for myself is directed against the heretics and against my accusers.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: How does it touch you who profess to be both an orthodox person and my admirer, if I am a little too sharp upon heretics, and expose their tricks before the public?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: You should rejoice in my invectives: otherwise, if you are vexed at them, you may be thought to be yourself a heretic.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: When anything is written against some particular vice, but without the mention of any name, if a man grows angry he accuses himself.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: It would have been the part of a wise man, even if he felt hurt, to dissemble his consciousness of wrong, and by the serenity of his countenance to dissipate the cloud that lay upon his heart.
